data.title: Arm: Strong AI Position, But Too High A Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM) is a tough investment. At its core, the business is not growing fast enough to get rewarded with a "hyper growth" label. In fact, I believe that after this upcoming quarter, its revenue growth rates are likely to decelerate as it comes up against more challenging comparables.

Meanwhile, the stock is still priced way higher than 150x forward free cash flow. This is just too much of an ask. Therefore, I remain neutral on ARM.

Quick Recap of My Position

Here you can read more about my previous views on Arm.

Arm's Near-Term Prospects

Arm builds the ''blueprints'' for computer chips that power pretty much everything—from smartphones to laptops and even cloud data centers.

Arm’s designs aim to deliver high performance while keeping power consumption low. That’s a huge deal in the age of AI, where workloads are exploding and efficiency is key.

Tech giants like Apple (AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), NVIDIA (NVDA), and AWS (AMZN) all rely on Arm’s platform to deliver faster and more energy-efficient devices and servers.

That said, smartphone demand has not been that strong, plus, growth comps get tougher after the next quarter.

At around 20% revenue growth, Arm could start looking less like a “hyper-growth” story.

On top of that, while Arm has carved out a strong niche in AI, it still faces heavyweight rivals like Intel’s (INTC) x86 architecture and AMD (AMD) in the data center, plus custom silicon efforts from the big cloud providers themselves.

So, it's not an easy setup for investors.

Revenue Growth Rates May Stabilize at 20%

Arm’s upcoming quarter will likely be its easiest comparable, which should make for a strong headline growth number. But beyond that, the next few quarters will be measured against much tougher comps. That sets the stage for revenue growth to settle closer to ~20% y/y, rather than the outsized figures some investors may be hoping for.

The problem is that ~20% y/y revenue growth isn’t the kind of number that convinces investors they’re looking at a true growth story. For a stock priced at such a premium, the market expects acceleration, not deceleration. Anything less than sustained 30%+ growth will struggle to justify Arm’s valuation and leaves little room for error in the narrative.

ARM Stock Valuation -- 204x Forward Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I avoid companies with flimsy balance sheets. Funny thing is, most investors don’t think twice about balance sheets—until the day it’s all anyone can think about. I prefer to be ahead of that curve.

On this front, Arm operates debt-free, which gives the business a lot of flexibility. Yes, admittedly, Arm holds under 2% of its market cap as cash, thereby it falls short of my desired criteria of 5% of my cash to market ratio.

Next, we know that Arm's free cash flow for the trailing twelve months was approximately $600 million, down from the roughly $700 million delivered in the prior twelve-month rolling period.

Accordingly, if we presume that over the next twelve months, Arm is able to increase its free cash flow by 25% y/y, this would get its free cash flow to around $750 million.

This is a steady bump from where it is right now, and yet, only a little higher than where it was in the past. In other words, assuming revenue grows by roughly 20% y/y, together with cost efficiencies and scale could make that 25% y/y FCF growth achievable.

This leaves the stock priced at approximately 204x forward free cash flow. However, we cut the story up, paying such a high free cash flow multiple leaves me with absolutely no margin of safety.

As a rule, I prefer an adjusted PEG ratio (using FCF instead of earnings) of under 3x. Right now, Arm sits closer to 10x. Maybe that drops to ~7x if investors get comfortable with 30%+ revenue growth next year—but that’s a big stretch.

Risk Factors to the Upside

The bulk of my concern is that the business is very highly valued, and yet, it delivers a lacklustre amount of free cash flow. If Arm were able to significantly improve its earnings to free cash flow potential, perhaps getting closer to $1 billion, this would dispel any bearish argument facing the stock.

Plus, as mentioned already, another risk factor towards the upside would be if Arm could grow its revenue line at a rate higher than 30% y/y. If investors could get confident that higher than 30% was the ''new normal, this would banish the bears in Arm's corner.

The Bottom Line

In conclusion, I find Arm to be too close to fully valued to leave me with much upside.

Think about it, the stock is already trading at more than 200x forward free cash flow, while revenue growth looks set to decelerate to perhaps around 20% y/y. Even at 25% y/y I would struggle to get bullish on Arm.

Yes, the business is debt-free. Also, it has a strong position in AI, but with free cash flow still hovering around, say $650 to 750 million and comps getting tougher, I just don’t see enough financial strength or growth momentum to justify this kind of premium.

Inflection investing is about backing companies where next year is likely to be better than this year. Not the reverse. No matter the story.

As such, I remain neutral on Arm as I have been for quite some time.

data.title: British American Tobacco: The Equity Story Has Been Exhausted (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I have been covering and carrying a long position in British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) since February 2024. At that time, when I built a notable stake in BTI, the case for me, as for an income-focused investor, seemed a 'no-brainer.' Apart from offering almost a double-digit yield, it ticked all of the necessary boxes for being considered a high and durable passive income pick, such as:

  • P/CF multiple at a low single-digit territory.
  • Investment-grade balance sheet.
  • Significant surplus in cash generation after the dividend distributions.
  • Relatively defensive business segment.
  • Cash-flow-focused management (i.e., no major/risky CapEx projects in the pipeline).
  • Stable dividend-paying track-record for more than 25 years in a row.

In a nutshell, by going long BTI, I was confident of two things: 1) receiving tangible and stress-free dividends, 2) sooner or later benefiting from valuation-driven (recovery process) price appreciation.

Since the publication of my original piece in early 2024, the stock has produced ~93% in total returns.

Going into 2025, I issued a follow-up article to address the significant stock price inflation aspect, and, effectively, to understand whether the thesis remains intact. The title of that piece says it all: British American Tobacco: My Largest Traditional Stock, This Is Why. After digesting the updated financials and putting that into the context of BTI's multiple and yield, my conclusion was that there was still some gas left in the tank for BTI to surprise investors to the upside.

The case has worked out smoothly as well with BTI registering a total return performance of ~43%.

While all of this is great, and, personally, I am happy to see that an income-focused stock has delivered and outperformed the booming markets such as the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), I have legitimate concerns about the future potential for BTI.

We have to understand that the lion's share of BTI's returns has stemmed from the recovery in previously very depressed levels. As a result of the higher valuation multiple, it is only natural that we see a much smaller yield now. Currently, BTI offers a dividend yield of 5.8%.

With this in mind, let me refresh my thesis on BTI.

Thesis update

I will start with a couple of facts covering the valuation front.

If we compare BTI's current valuations (using almost any metric) to its 5-year average levels, then we will notice 15% to 25% overvaluation, depending on the metric we choose. On a P/E basis, the difference lies closer to the upper range. In terms of the dividend yield, the situation does not look too expensive compared to the pre-COVID era, but relative to the past 5-year period, the yield is obviously not as attractive anymore

The valuation multiple that I like to use and what is usually applied in official valuation works by consultants and/or investment bankers is the EV/EBITDA. So, as of now, BTI trades at an EV/EBITDA of 9.5x, which is largely in line with quite many high-quality income-focused asset classes (securities).

I will give you two quick examples.

1. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP) (BIPC) - an investment-grade, infrastructure-focused business offering ~5.5% yield and a similar level of annual dividend growth (at EV/EBITDA of 10.5x). See more details about the pick in my article here.

2. Energy Transfer (ET) - an investment-grade, midstream focused business offering a ~7.5% yield and a ~3% annual dividend growth (at EV/EBITDA of 9.2x). See more details about the pick in my article here.

I think that it is pretty clear that BTI does not anymore carry a deep bargain case that could be scooped up with an expectation of enjoying strong returns from some valuation multiple recovery process.

In my opinion, this does not mean that BTI is a bad investment or that investors should fire-sale the position. Instead, what it means is that we have to now be more focused on BTI's value (and risk) underlying drivers, as there is no longer an abnormal embedded margin of safety that would offset (neutralize) any struggles that BTI might experience in its financials. Namely, the bar for BTI to deliver strong returns has been set much higher than before.

The consensus estimates on BTI's EPS generation going forward indicate the same thing. For BTI, which operates in a mature and in some areas even declining business, achieving ~6% growth for two years in a row (2026 and 2027) is indeed a difficult task, to say the least.

Let's unpack this assumption a bit.

Over the past 5-year period, BTI has managed to expand EBITDA by ~4.5%, which is less than what has been baked into the cake for 2026 and 2027 (for EPS). Historical 5-year EPS growth has landed at roughly the same level of ~4.6%. So, the current expectation means that BTI has to find additional avenues through which to not only maintain the tempo but also enhance it by a rate that is actually quite tangible for any mature value business.

In my opinion, it will be extremely difficult for BTI to achieve this.

Here is why.

A large part of the realized EPS growth has stemmed from aggressive buybacks, which have been fueled by some extraordinary items like divestitures and equity stake monetization in ICT. For example, relatively recently BTI sold a decent chunk of ICT, where the proceeds were directed towards bumping up the buyback program from ~$260 million to $1.5 billion (I have converted from pounds to USD). Some part of these proceeds was also parked at the balance sheet level in order to de-leverage the business.

We have to appreciate that the selected equity stake monetizations are inherently one-offs, which cannot happen again and again going forward. In practice, BTI will have to find fresh EBITDA sources to both replace the one-off item drivers and secure incremental growth.

Looking at the current financials, the story does not look that encouraging in the context of meeting the aforementioned targets. The H1 results indicate that the BTI as a group has managed to grow the top line by only 1%. On the operating cash flow level, the growth has been better, but not even close to what is expected (at ~2%).

My key concerns lie in the conventional cigarette segment, which has remained under pressure for many years in a row, and shows no signs of stopping. For example, in H1, the YTD sales were down by ~11%. And let's not forget that this particular segment accounts for a notable part of BTI's total EBITDA generation.

Granted, there are new categories, which are offsetting the negatives associated with combustibles, but the question is how durable the new category trend is. In H1 the vapor segment had actually declined. In heated products, the gross volume share for BIT was down by 0.9% in top markets.

Furthermore, as a prudent investor, I would operate with an assumption that BTI's core business will continue to face structural headwinds from increased taxation, restrictions, and mushrooming illegal product sales. For example, it is hard to imagine how governments across the world would not explore ways to boost revenues from such a populistic area like tobacco taxation (especially, given the mounting deficits).

The bottom line

In nutshell, BTI is a hold for me, and not a buy.

There are two major reasons why I have stopped adding more to BTI's position (i.e., canceled the DRIP plan).

First, the dividend yield of 5.8% is not that attractive anymore, especially compared to other durable and sound alternatives such as ET and BIP, which yield the same or even more and at the same time offer better growth prospects (and inherently more defensive business models).

Second, the current multiple is underpinned by too optimistic growth assumptions. In my humble opinion, in the best case, BTI could deliver on the "priced in" EPS trajectory, which would basically justify a somewhat flat share price. Yet, as outlined in the article above, my base assumption is that BTI falls short of the EPS consensus growth figures, which, in turn, would give a solid basis for the market to reprice the stock lower.
